segments.s327-g9: دگر is an adverb meaning 'moreover/again' introducing a new point. آن‌که‌گر is a fused relative/conditional phrase ('if it be that / if one') that opens a hypothetical clause. بشمری is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive form of شماردن here meaning 'you reckon/consider'; رنج اوی is the direct object 'his suffering' (رنج = suffering, اوی = his), with the possessor following the noun as in classical Persian.
segments.s327-g10: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly'. فزون آید is a light-verb construction in which the adjective/noun فزون 'more/greater' combines with آید ('comes' / 'will be') to mean 'will be greater'. از introduces the comparison 'than' (literally 'from'), followed by گنج اوی 'his treasure'. Word order places the comparative phrase before the comparison phrase.
segments.s327-g11: سپر کرد is a causative/completing phrase 'made (into) a shield' where کرد is the past tense 'did/made'. پیشم = 'before me / in front of me'. تن خویش را is 'his own body' formed from تن (body) + خویش (own) where the possessive clitic ش is attached; را is the definite object marker. The verb (کرد) governs the object 'تن خویش را'.
segments.s327-g12: نبد is an archaic negative-existential/past form 'there was not / did not have'. خواب و خوردن are coordinated nouns 'sleep and eating/food'. بداندیش را ('for the ill-wisher') is the beneficiary/possessor marked with را; the construction means 'for the ill-wisher there was no sleep or food.' The verb position before the objects is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s327-g13: همان پاسخت را places the demonstrative/emphatic 'that same answer' before the verb; را is the object marker. به خوبی کنیم uses the preposition به + abstract noun خوبی 'kindness' plus the verb کنیم (1st-person plural subjunctive/optative) meaning 'we shall do / we will render' — together 'we will repay/answer that with kindness.' The verb form کنيم is a hortative/intentional form rather than simple present.
segments.s327-g14: دلت را is 'your heart' formed from دل + 2nd-person suffix ت, followed by the object marker را. به گفتار تو is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by/with your words' (به + گفتار + تو). نشکنیم is the negative present/subjunctive (ن + شکنیم) 'we will not break.' The negative prefix ن attaches to the verb, and the clause states a deliberate refusal to cause emotional harm.
segments.s327-g15: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus he said' where چنین ('thus') modifies گفت (here the past-tense verb 'he said'). زآن پس ('after that') gives temporal sequence. به آواز سخت is a manner phrase: به + آواز ('in/with a voice') and سخت functions adverbially 'loudly/firmly/strongly' — together 'he said in a loud/harsh voice.'
segments.s327-g16: که‌ای here begins the reported speech and contains the vocative particle ای (rendered attached to که in the manuscript), functioning like 'O' addressing the addressees. سرفرازان پیروز بخت is a string of epithets: سرفرازان 'proud/exalted ones' (plural), پیروز 'victorious', and بخت 'fortune/fate' — together a vocative 'O proud, victorious ones of fortune.' Word order is epithets followed by the noun 'fortune' as a qualifying attribute.
segments.s327-g73: This is a vocative address: تو‌ای ('O you') + a string of epithets that precede the name. پیر 'old/elder' and بیدار 'awake/alert' are adjectival epithets before دستان, here an epithet formed from 'hands' (i.e. 'broad/forceful‑handed'), all modifying the proper name سام 'Sam'. Word order (epithets before the name) is typical of classical Persian poetic style.
segments.s327-g74: مرا ... گویی functions as 'you call me / you say of me' (مرا = 'me' as direct object; را is the object marker attached here to the previous element). گویی is a poetic present/imperative form meaning 'you say' or 'you call (it)'. The relative clause که بنهاد دام 'who laid a trap' follows to describe the predicate 'demon': که introduces the clause, بنهاد is an old past form 'put/laid', and دام = 'trap'.
segments.s327-g75: به + noun(s) marks direction/into: به تاری و کژی = 'into darkness and crookedness'. بگشتم is the speaker's verb 'I turned / I was turned' (poetic past), and ز راه 'from the road' shows separation/straying. The sequence names the moral/spiritual directions the speaker has gone into.
segments.s327-g76: روان گشته = 'the soul has become' (روان 'soul/spirit' + گشته, the perfect/participial form of گشتن used as 'became'). بی‌مایه is an adjectival predicate 'without worth/worthless', and دل تباه is a parallel nominal phrase 'the heart ruined' (دل 'heart' + تباه 'ruined'). The participle + adjective/noun pattern expresses the resultant state.
segments.s327-g77: ندانم is the negative present 'I do not know' (from دانستن). که introduces an indirect question/clause. بادافره is a compound adjective/noun 'wind‑borne' or 'wind‑borne one' and ایزدی 'divine / god‑sent' modifies it; together they form a descriptive noun phrase ('a god‑sent, wind‑borne [thing/person/help]') whose precise head is left implicit but understood from context.
segments.s327-g78: کجا یابم is the indirect-question phrase 'where I may find / where shall I find' (کجا 'where' + یابم, subjunctive/optative or present form of یافتن used in questions). و links the next clause, روزگار بدی = 'bad fortune/times' (روزگار 'fate/times' + بدی 'bad/evil'), a short statement about the speaker's ill condition.
segments.s327-g57: دگر 'another/other' opens the clause (here 'as for the other'). آن‌که 'the one who' introduces a relative clause. گفتی is the 2nd-person singular past of گفتن ('you said/you named'), so the phrase means 'the other that you mentioned'. که is the complementizer 'that'. با + noun marks the adversary/companion 'with'; شیده is the noun object of با (here an epithet meaning a wild/mad one). جنگ is a noun 'battle/fight' serving as the action associated with شیده ('who made/was in fight with the shīdeh').
segments.s327-g58: بیاراستی here functions as an adverbial intensifier 'truly/in truth' (archaic/poetic). چون starts the simile 'like/as'. دلاور is an adjective/noun 'valiant, brave', and پلنگ is the simile's image 'leopard'. Together چـون دلاور پلنگ modifies the preceding clause: 'he was truly like a brave leopard.'
segments.s327-g59: از آن بد 'worse than that' is a formulaic turn ('از آن بد' = 'worse than that'). کز is the contracted form of 'که از' or a relative 'that/which', here introducing the content of the complaint: ایران ندیدم سوار = 'I saw no rider from Iran'. ندیدم is the 1st-person past negative 'I did not see'. سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman' and the logical object of ندیدم; ایران marks origin ('from Iran').
segments.s327-g60: نه ... نه ... is a negation pattern 'neither ... nor ...'. اسپ افگنی is a compound: اسپ 'steed/horse' and افگنی 'shining/radiant' (an epithet). از در کارزار literally 'from/in the field of battle' — از + در here indicates source/location in older poetic usage, and کارزار is 'battlefield/battle'. The whole phrase denies the presence of even a shining horse in battle.
segments.s327-g61: که is a clause introducer ('for/that'). تنها 'alone/only' modifies the subject (you). بر او 'against him' uses the preposition بر meaning 'against/upon'. به جنگ آمدی is a past-tense verbal phrase 'you came to battle' (آمدی = 2nd-person past 'you came'), so the segment means 'that you came against him alone into battle.'
segments.s327-g62: چو here means 'when'. رفتی is 2nd-person past 'you went/you left'. به رزمش 'into his combat/contest' (رزم + possessive -ش). درنگ آمدی is an impersonal/result clause: درنگ 'hesitation/delay' + آمدی 'came' yields 'hesitation came upon you' (i.e., you became hesitant). The order is poetic: cause/time clause + result ('when you went ... hesitation came').
segments.s327-g63: کسی را کجا ... نبود is a rhetorical negative construction: 'Where was there anyone ... not?' را is the object/topic marker attaching emphasis to کسی. فر یزدان is a compound meaning 'divine farr/royal radiance' (فر = royal glory, یزدان = the divine). نبود is past negative 'was not'. The whole means roughly 'There was no one for whom the divine farr of God was absent.'
segments.s327-g64: وگر is 'and if' / 'but if'. اختر نیک 'the good/auspicious star' (اختر = star, نیک here = auspicious). خندان 'smiling' is a frequent poetic image for a favorable star. نبود is again past negative 'was not'. The clause is conditional/elliptical ('and if the auspicious star had not smiled...'), typically continued or implying consequence in the following lines.
segments.s327-g1: چو is a conjunction here meaning "when"; the clause is a temporal subordinate: "چو کیخسرو آن گفت ایشان شنید" = "when Kay Khosrow said that, they heard." آن is the demonstrative object "that" placed before the verb گفت (past tense of گفتن, 'said'). ایشان is the subject "they" and شنید is the past tense verb "heard."
segments.s327-g2: زمانی is an adverbial phrase "for a while"; بیاسود here is used adjectivally/verbally to mean "was not at ease/uneasy." و is the coordinating conjunction "and." اندر is a preposition/adverb "within/inwardly" and شمید is a poetic past/presentive verb meaning "perceived" or "felt/plumbed inwardly." The sequence describes a brief pause and inner apprehension.
segments.s327-g3: پراندیشه is an epithet/adjective meaning "full of thought/pensive" describing the speaker; it precedes the verb. گفت is the past-tense verb "said" (here followed by a direct address). ای introduces a vocative "O!" and جهاندیده is a compound epithet "world‑wise" addressing زال (the proper name). The line sets up a direct address to Zal.
segments.s327-g4: به here functions as a preposition meaning roughly "to/for/in the case of" introducing the description of a man. مردی is the indefinite/partitive "a man," بی‌اندازه is an adverb "beyond measure/excessively," and پیموده is a verbal/adjectival form "having traversed/experienced" that modifies سال ("years"). The phrase = "a man who has traversed countless years."
segments.s327-g5: اگر is the conditional "if." سرد modifies گویمت and here means "cold/harsh" (i.e., bluntly); گویمت is the 1st-person present/future-like form "I tell you/I say" (used for a hypothetical speech act). بر is a preposition here meaning "before/on (the occasion of)" and انجمن = "assembly/council." Together: "If I say it bluntly before the council..."
segments.s327-g6: جهاندار is a title "the world‑lord/sovereign" (the king). نپسندد is the negative present/subjunctive of پسندیدن, here best translated "will not approve/like." این refers to "this (matter)" and بد means "bad/unfavourable." ز is the poetic preposition "from/of" and من is the first‑person pronoun "me." The final phrase conveys "the king will disapprove; this would reflect badly on me."
segments.s327-g7: دگر آن‌که is a common formula meaning "another thing is that" or "moreover, that..." رستم is the proper name Rostam (subject). شود is the present/past-form of شدن "becomes/happens" and دردمند is a predicate adjective "sorrowful/afflicted." The clause states the alternative possibility that Rostam falls into distress.
segments.s327-g8: ز is the poetic preposition "from/because of." درد is "pain/affliction" and وی refers back to Rostam ("his"). آید is the verb "comes/happens" and به ایران is the prepositional phrase "to/for Iran." گزند is the noun "harm/damage"—the thing that will come. Word order is poetic: "From his pain comes to Iran harm."
segments.s327-g49: هرآنگه که is a compound conjunction meaning 'whenever/when'; اندیشه is the subject ('thought' or 'thinking') and گرد(د) here is the present/subjunctive/passive‑style verb 'become' (گردد = 'becomes'); دراز is a predicative adjective placed after the verb ('long, prolonged'). The clause literally reads 'whenever thinking becomes long.'
segments.s327-g50: ز شادی و از دولت دیریاز are two parallel prepositional phrases giving the cause/ground: both ز and از mean 'from' (poetic), شادی = 'joy', دولت = 'fortune/prosperity', and دیریاز is a compound adjective 'long‑standing/long‑lasting' modifying دولت. The repetition (ز ... و از ...) is a poetic coordination.
segments.s327-g51: چو here = 'as/like' (introducing comparison/condition); the proper names کاووس و جمشید follow. باشم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of بودن ('to be') used here in a conditional sense ('if I am' or 'were I to be'); به راه is a locative phrase 'on the road' or 'on a journey.'
segments.s327-g52: چو ایشان = 'like them' (reintroducing the comparison). ز من = 'from me' (i.e., 'away from me' or 'lost to me'); گم شود is a passive/intransitive pattern 'become lost' (شود = 'become'); پایگاه is the thing lost — 'base/stronghold/foothold.' The word order is poetic: object/predicate after the verb.
segments.s327-g53: چو = 'as/like' introducing the comparison; ضحاک ناپاک pairs a name with the adjective ناپاک ('impure/evil'), and و تور دلیر links the second name with دلیر ('brave'). This is a typical appositional/epithetic structure in epic diction.
segments.s327-g54: که here introduces the explanatory/causal clause ('because/for'), از جور ایشان = 'from/through their injustice/oppression', جهان = 'the world', گشت is the past/epic perfect of گشتن 'became/turned', and سیر is predicate adjective 'sated/filled/weary'. The sequence جهان گشت سیر = 'the world became sated.'
segments.s327-g55: بترسم is 'I fear' (present tense, first person). که introduces the feared clause 'that ...'. چون روز نخ برکشد is a temporal/figurative clause: چون = 'when'/'as', روز = 'day' (here personified or fate‑like), نخ = 'thread' (a life/thread metaphor), and برکشد (بر + کشد) = 'draws/pulls' (a verbal compound 'to pull/draw up'). The phrase expresses a poetic image of fate pulling a life thread.
segments.s327-g56: چو ایشان = 'as/like them' (comparison). مرا is the object 'me' (here shown tokenized as م + را in the line; م corresponds to the pronoun and را is the object marker). سوی = 'toward', دوزخ = 'Hell', and کشد is intended as 'pulls/drags (toward)' — the verb here acts on مرا as its object. Note: in the provided word list 'م' and 'کشد' lack matching sense entries for the pronoun and the verb sense 'to drag', so those are flagged below accordingly.
segments.s327-g17: سخنهای دستان is a genitive/possessive grouping: سخنهای (plural 'words/tales') governed by دستان (here 'dastān' = 'tales/storytellers'). شنیدم is the simple past 1st-person singular 'I heard'. همه after the verb emphasizes 'all/everything' (object scope). Word order (V + object phrase + quantifier) is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s327-g18: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'that'. بیدار here functions as a substantive/adjective 'the watchful/awake one' (agent of the following verb). بگشاد is an archaic poetic past form 'opened' (3rd sg.). پیش is a preposition 'in front of / before', and رمه is its object 'the flock/herd'.
segments.s327-g19: به here functions as a prepositional/introducing particle used in an address ('to/for' — vocative sense in poetry). دارنده یزدان گیهان is a compound epithet: دارنده 'possessor/holder' governed by یزدان 'God' and modified by گیهان 'of the world' (i.e. 'holder endowed by God for the world' or 'possessor of God's world'). خدیو is the addressee/title ('Khedive' = ruler) placed at the end.
segments.s327-g20: که introduces the content clause 'that / namely'. من دورم is present-tense copular 'I am distant' (دورم = دور + ـم). از marks separation 'from', and راه و فرمان coordinate two nouns 'way/path and command/order'. دیو is the genitive governor 'of the demon' ('the devil's way and command').
segments.s327-g21: به یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'to/unto God'. گراید is a present/habitual poetic verb 'turns/inclines' (3rd sing. form), and همی is an emphatic adverb 'ever/always' placed after the verb (typical in older Persian). جان من is the possessed noun 'my life/soul' — the logical subject that 'turns' toward God.
segments.s327-g22: که links this clause as explanation 'for/that'. آن دیدم is past first-person 'I saw that' (آن = 'that'). از here introduces the relation 'as/from' and رنج درمان من is a genitive phrase 'the remedy/cure of my suffering' (درمان من = 'my cure'). The clause states the reason: he turned to God because he perceived it as his cure.
segments.s327-g23: بدید is the past form 'saw' (3rd sing. here used in narrative; verb placed first as a poetic inversion). آن جهان را contains the object ('that world' marked by را). دل روشنم is an appositive/subject phrase 'my heart, enlightened' (روشن with the possessive suffix -م), positioned after the verb for emphasis: 'my enlightened heart saw that world.'
segments.s327-g24: خرد شد = 'wisdom became' (خرد 'wisdom' as subject + شد 'became'). ز introduces the cause/agent 'because of/from' and بدهای او 'his evils' is the cause. جوشنم is 'my cuirass/armor' (جوشن + possessive -م). The clause means 'my wisdom turned into my armor against his evils.'
segments.s327-g41: چنان دان is an instruction/imperatival phrase: چنان (so/thus) + دان (know/consider). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). اندر فزونی منش is the clause complement: اندر (in/within) + فزونی (excess/abundance) + منش (disposition/character). The whole segment: 'consider it so that in abundance [they do not adopt a certain disposition]'.
segments.s327-g42: نسازند is negative present/subjunctive 3rd plural of ساختن/ساخت (نَ + سازند) meaning 'they do not make/adopt'. بر here means 'against' (used with سرزنش), followed by the object پادشا (king). سرزنش is a noun 'reproach'. Word order is verb after subordinate clause: 'that ... they do not make reproach against the king.'
segments.s327-g43: کنون من begins the time-setting 'now I'. چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. کین پدر خواستم is 'I desired/sought vengeance for my father' — خواستم is past tense 'I wanted/sought'. The clause reads: 'Now, when I sought my father's vengeance...'.
segments.s327-g44: جهان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker: 'the world'. به پیروزی is an instrumental/means phrase ('by/with victory') and آراستم is a first-person past 'I adorned/arrayed'. The verb completes the previous temporal clause: '...I adorned the world with victory.'
segments.s327-g45: بکشتم is an archaic/past first-person form 'I slew' (here functioning as the main past verb). کسی را is the object 'someone'. کز (a contracted relative 'که + ز') introduces a relative clause 'who/from whom', او بود کین = 'who had enmity'. بود is past 'was'. The whole segment: 'I killed someone who bore him hatred/vengeance.'
segments.s327-g46: وز here functions as the poetic/pre-classical preposition 'از' (from/of) introducing source: 'from him'. او is the source pronoun. جور و بیداد بد is a piled-up nominal phrase 'injustice and oppression (cruel/bad)'; بد qualifies the preceding nouns. بر زمین is a locative 'upon the earth/land'. The clause says: 'and from him came cruel injustice and oppression upon the earth.'
segments.s327-g47: به گیتی sets the scene 'in/to the world'. مرا نیز is 'for me also' (مرا = me; نیز = also). کاری نماند uses کاری as 'a deed/thing to do' and نماند (past) meaning 'did not remain/was left'. The idiom 'کاری نماند' = 'no work/achievement remained'.
segments.s327-g48: ز is the poetic 'from'. بدگوهران ('bad‑gem/ill‑natured ones') refers to people of bad character; یادگاری is 'memorial/memento' and نماند again 'did not remain'. Together: 'from the ill‑natured no memorial remained.'
segments.s327-g25: به + proper name marks the indirect object (‘to Zal’). آنگهی is an adverb meaning ‘then/thereupon’ and typically sits after the indirect object for emphasis. گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’; تندی مکن is a prohibitive clause: تندی (harshness/fierceness) is the noun-object and مکن is the negative imperative/auxiliary ‘do not (do)’. The whole segment is ‘He said to Zal then: do not be harsh.’
segments.s327-g26: بر اندازه is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘within measure / in moderation’ (بر + اندازه). باید is a modal/impersonal ‘it is necessary / one must’. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. رانی here is an archaic/personal-form verb meaning ‘(you) speak / utter’ (second-person form used with باید), and سخن is its object ‘speech’. The clause thus means ‘you must speak in measure.’
segments.s327-g27: نخست is an adverb ‘first/at first’ placed before the clause. آن + noun (دکه here) is a demonstrative phrase ‘that X’. گفتی is a past tense form (second-person past in classical Persian: ‘you said’) used to report the earlier speech. ز is the poetic preposition ‘of/from’, leading into the origin phrase.
segments.s327-g28: This short predicate string gives qualities or a verdict about the referent: خردمند و بیدار are coordinated adjectives (‘wise and alert’). هرگز is the negative adverb ‘never’ and precedes نزاد, whose root is زاد (to be born / to be produced); here نزاد functions to mean ‘was not born / did not arise’ or more idiomatically ‘was not of that stock’. The word order places the negation before the verbal root as typical in poetry.
segments.s327-g29: This is a nominal identification: جهاندار (title/name) followed by پور + personal name as a patronymic (‘son of Siyavash’). منم is the enclitic copula ‘I am’ (archaic poetic form of من هستم), closing the nominal identification.
segments.s327-g30: ز تخم means ‘from the seed/lineage of…’ and takes the following genitive: کیان راد is a compound indicating the Kian (royal) stock qualified by راد (‘noble/generous’). و باهش منم contains باهش (‘with it/him’, a preposition + enclitic pronoun) plus another copular منم, literally ‘and I am with it/with him’ — i.e. ‘and I too belong to that noble stock / stand with it.’
segments.s327-g31: نبیره is a noun meaning ‘offspring / scion’ and is followed by an appositive noun phrase جهاندار کاووس (‘the world‑ruler Kavus’). کی here is a relative pronoun (‘who’) linking this noun phrase to the adjectival predicates that follow.
segments.s327-g32: A string of coordinated predicates describing the relative: دل‌افروز (‘delightful / heart‑pleasing’), و با دانش (‘and with learning’ — a prepositional adjective meaning ‘learned’), و نیک‌پی (‘and well‑born’). The adjectives are tied to the prior relative كي and form a descriptive clause modifying the scion.
segments.s327-g65: همه خاک بودی is an idiomatic past statement: همه (completely/wholly) modifies خاک بودی, where خاک بودن means 'to be reduced to dust / utterly defeated'. بودی is the 2nd-person singular past 'you were'. به جنگ پشنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in the battle of Pashang' (به + noun), with پشنگ a proper name.
segments.s327-g66: از ایران is a simple 'from Iran' phrase. بدین سان is a fixed idiom meaning 'in this way / thus' (here written broken into parts); شدم is 1st-person past 'I became'. تیزچنگ is a proper name (literally 'sharp-clawed') and serves as the predicate/identity that the speaker takes on.
segments.s327-g67: بدین پنج هفته places a duration ('for these five weeks' / 'for five weeks in this manner'); که introduces the clause that follows ('that' / 'during which'). من روز و شب gives the subject 'I' plus the adverbial phrase 'day and night' specifying the time frame for the action.
segments.s327-g68: همی before the verb expresses continuous or habitual action ('was always / kept on'). بآفرین functions adverbially as 'with praise' or calling out praise. برگشادم is the 1st-person past 'I opened' (برگشاد + -م), and دو لب ('two lips') is the direct object placed after the verb.
segments.s327-g69: بدان تا is a classical phrasing introducing a wish or purpose clause; تا here functions as 'so that / in order that'. جهاندار یزدان پاک is a stacked title/epithet: جهاندار 'world-holder/sovereign', یزدان 'God', پاک 'pure' (adjective modifying یزدان).
segments.s327-g70: رهاند is a 3rd-person form meaning 'may free / deliver' in this optative/wishful context (bound to the preceding تا). مرا is the object 'me' (here split in the list as م + را: م = me-clitic, را = definite/direct-object marker). ز is the classical preposition 'from', and این غم تیره خاک is the noun phrase 'this dark sorrow of the earth', the thing from which deliverance is sought.
segments.s327-g71: شدم سیر is a verbal phrase: شدم (1st-person past 'I became') + سیر (sated/wearied) = 'I became sated/grew weary'. ز این لشکر و تاج و تخت uses ز 'from/of' to give the cause/objects of weariness: 'this army and crown and throne' (a list linked by و).
segments.s327-g72: سبک‌بار is an adjective/compound 'light-burdened / light in load' and گشتیم is past plural 'we became' or 'we turned' (here 'we made ourselves light'). و connects the clauses, بستیم is past plural 'we bound/closed' (from بستن), and رخت ('baggage/gear/clothes') is the object: 'we lightened our loads and packed up our belongings.'
segments.s327-g33: This is a prepositional/dative phrase: 'به مادر هم' = 'even to/for the mother' or idiomatically 'even his mother'. It is followed by an origin phrase 'از تخم افراسیاب' where 'تخم' is used figuratively for 'seed/stock, origin' (lineage) and 'افراسیاب' is the proper name. Word order is poetic: dative/beneficiary + origin, with no ezafe explicitly shown.
segments.s327-g34: 'که' introduces a relative clause modifying 'مادر'. 'با خشم او' is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/with his anger'. The verb cluster 'گم شدی' is a classical past/resultative meaning 'became lost/was lost' (the -ی ending is an archaic/poetic inflection). The nouns 'خورد و خواب' form a fixed pair 'eating and sleeping' (here 'خورد' functions as the noun 'eating'), so the clause means she 'lost eating and sleeping' (i.e. lost appetite and sleep) because of his wrath.
segments.s327-g35: A coordinated nominal phrase naming lineage: 'نبیره فریدون' (scion/offspring of Fereydun) and 'پور پشنگ' (son/descendant of Pashang). 'نبیره' and 'پور' are descent labels; the proper names follow them without an explicit ezafe in this poetic register, and 'و' links the two appositive phrases.
segments.s327-g36: 'از این گوهران' literally 'from these jewels' (used metaphorically for this noble company). 'چنین' (= 'such') modifies 'ننگ'. The copula 'نیست' follows 'چنین' and precedes the noun 'ننگ' in a poetic inversion: 'چنین نیست ننگ' = 'there is no such shame'. Word order is marked by poetic inversion but the logical sense is 'among these jewels such a shame does not exist.'
segments.s327-g37: 'که' introduces this clause. 'شیران ایران' is the subject ('lions of Iran' — here metaphorical for brave men). The prepositional phrase 'به دریای آب' ('into/into the sea of water') is placed before the verb for poetic order; note 'به' here has the directional/into sense.
segments.s327-g38: 'نشستی' is the past tense (poetic form) of 'شستن' meaning 'washed/bathed'. 'تن' (body) is the object: 'washed their bodies'. 'از بیم افراسیاب' is a causal prepositional phrase: here 'از' = 'because of / out of (fear)'. So the whole clause reads 'they washed their bodies in the sea because of fear of Afrasiab.'
segments.s327-g39: 'دگر آن‌که' = 'the other, namely the one who...' introduces an appositive/relative idea. 'کاووس صندوق ساخت' is a simple past clause: subject 'کاووس' + object 'صندوق' (a chest) + verb 'ساخت' ('made'). In Persian poetry the relative is compact: 'the other, the one for whom Kaavus made a chest.'
segments.s327-g40: 'سر از پادشاهی همی برفراخت' is idiomatic/poetic: 'سر' (head) is what is lifted; 'از پادشاهی' marks the sphere/source ('into/onto kingship' in sense). 'همی' is an archaic/poetic aspectual particle (continuous or emphatic in classical Persian), and 'برفراخت' (past of 'فراختن') means 'raised/erected'. Together the phrase means he 'raised his head into/for kingship' — i.e., he elevated himself to royal status.

sentences.s327-s5: Moreover, if you reckon his suffering, truly it will exceed his treasure.
sentences.s327-s6: He made his body a shield before me; the ill‑wisher had neither sleep nor food.
sentences.s327-s7: We will repay that reply with kindness; we will not break your heart with your words.
sentences.s327-s8: Then he said in a loud voice thus: 'O proud and victorious ones of fortune!'
sentences.s327-s37: O old, alert‑handed Sam, you call me a demon who has set a snare.
sentences.s327-s38: I turned into darkness and crookedness, straying from the road; my soul has become worthless and my heart ruined.
sentences.s327-s39: I do not know where I can find a god‑sent, wind‑borne (help), and my fortune is evil.
sentences.s327-s29: As for the other one you spoke of, who fought with the shīdeh, he truly looked like a brave leopard.
sentences.s327-s30: Worse than that, I saw no rider from Iran, nor a shining steed on the battlefield.
sentences.s327-s31: For you alone came against him to fight; when you entered his combat you were struck by hesitation.
sentences.s327-s32: There was no one whose divine glory was lacking; and if the auspicious star had not been smiling...
sentences.s327-s1: When Kay Khosrow said that, they heard; for a while he was uneasy and inwardly perceived.
sentences.s327-s2: Pensive, he said: "O world‑wise Zal—(to) a man who has traversed years beyond measure..."
sentences.s327-s3: If I tell it bluntly before the assembly, the sovereign will not approve it; this would be bad for me.
sentences.s327-s4: Another (thing) is that Rostam becomes afflicted; from his pain harm will come to Iran.
sentences.s327-s25: Whenever my thought grows long — from joy and from long‑lasting fortune.
sentences.s327-s26: If I, like Kavus and Jamshid, am on the road, if like them my stronghold is lost to me.
sentences.s327-s27: Just as with Zahhāk the impure and Tūr the brave — because of their tyranny the world became sated.
sentences.s327-s28: I fear that when the day draws the thread, it will, like them, drag me toward Hell.
sentences.s327-s9: I heard all the tales of the storytellers — that the watchful one opened (the way) before the flock.
sentences.s327-s10: O Khedive, holder of (or endowed by) God's world — I am far from the devil's way and command.
sentences.s327-s11: My soul ever turns to God, for I saw that as the cure of my suffering.
sentences.s327-s12: My enlightened heart saw that world; my wisdom became, because of his evils, my cuirass (my protection).
sentences.s327-s21: Consider it so that, in times of abundance, they do not take to reproaching the king.
sentences.s327-s22: Now, when I sought vengeance for my father, I adorned the world with victory.
sentences.s327-s23: I slew one who bore him enmity; from him came cruel injustice and oppression upon the earth.
sentences.s327-s24: In the world for me no task remained either; from ill‑natured ones no memorial remained.
sentences.s327-s13: Then he said to Zal, "Do not be harsh; you must speak in measure."
sentences.s327-s14: At first you said that that one was of Turanian stock.
sentences.s327-s15: I am Jahandar, son of Siyavash; I descend from the Kian seed, noble, and I am with it.
sentences.s327-s16: A scion of the world-ruler Kavus, who is heart-pleasing, learned, and well-born.
sentences.s327-s33: You were utterly dust in the battle with Pashang; I came out of Iran and thus became Tizchang.
sentences.s327-s34: For five such weeks I, day and night, kept always opening my two lips in praise.
sentences.s327-s35: So that the sovereign, the pure God, may free me from this dark, earthly grief.
sentences.s327-s36: I grew weary of this army and of crown and throne; we lightened our burdens and packed up our gear.
sentences.s327-s17: Even his mother — of Afrasiab's seed — lost both eating and sleeping because of his wrath.
sentences.s327-s18: A scion of Fereydun and son of Pashang — among these jewels, there is no such shame.
sentences.s327-s19: That the lions of Iran washed their bodies in the sea of water for fear of Afrasiab.
sentences.s327-s20: The other one — the man for whom Kaavus made a chest — lifted his head into kingship.
